name: Mirror dark matter
summary: Postulates an exact (or near-exact) duplicate of the Standard Model with an unbroken Z_2 mirror symmetry, interacting with our sector only via gravity and (in some variants) photon-mirror-photon kinetic mixing. Mirror baryons constitute the dark matter; the mirror QCD scale, electroweak scale, and structure-formation properties are inherited from the SM. Distinct from twin Higgs in that mirror DM doesn't address the hierarchy problem on its own.

addresses_puzzles.note: Naturally produces a dark sector with self-interactions and structure formation similar to baryons; explains the cosmic coincidence Omega_DM/Omega_b ~ 5 if the mirror sector is slightly cooler. Cosmological constraints (BBN N_eff, CMB) require asymmetric reheating.

touches_benchmarks.note: Naive mirror model with equal SM and mirror temperatures violates Delta N_eff ~ 0.3 bound; viable mirror DM requires asymmetric inflaton decay or a temperature ratio < 0.5.
Mirror photon - SM photon kinetic mixing epsilon ~ 10^-9 - 10^-8 produces a small dark-matter coupling to ordinary matter; targeted by direct-detection experiments at low recoil energies.
Mirror baryons self-interact via mirror QCD/EM with sigma/m comparable to ordinary baryon-baryon scattering at small velocities; cluster-merger bounds are non-trivial.

introduces.note: Z_2 fixes most parameters as twins of SM. New free parameters: temperature ratio, kinetic mixing, and any explicit Z_2 breaking.

history: Lee and Yang (1956) speculated about mirror parity; modern mirror dark matter framework developed by Foot, Lew, Volkas (1991); systematic phenomenology by Foot in the 2000s. Foot (2004) 'Experimental Implications of Mirror Matter-Type Dark Matter', IJMPA 19, 3807, summarises the framework. Mirror DM revives periodically in light of small-scale structure and direct-detection anomalies.

references.citation: Foot, Lew, Volkas, 'A model with fundamental improper space-time symmetries', Phys. Lett. B 272, 67 (1991)
Foot, 'Experimental implications of mirror matter-type dark matter', Int. J. Mod. Phys. A 19, 3807 (2004)
